title: Reacting to an Exogenous Shock Mid-Forecast: A Demo Model with AEMO Electricity Demand Data
text:
Data Scientist & Data Engineer

    Forecasting outcomes. Modelling risk. Shipping data products that hold up in production.

title: Reacting to an Exogenous Shock Mid-Forecast: A Demo Model with AEMO Electricity Demand Data
text:
Overview
Every demand forecast leans on one assumption: that the relationship you fit on past data still holds across the horizon you’re forecasting. COVID-19 broke that assumption for almost everyone. It was a textbook exogenous shock: it arrived mid-forecast and pulled actual demand away from anything a pre-2020 model would have predicted.
This is a self-contained demo built on public AEMO operational demand data for Victoria. It asks one narrow question: when your forecast experiences a shock mid-flight, will you know something strange is occurring? How? And how soon?
Spoiler: the shock that first disrupted 2020 energy demand forecasts wasn’t COVID.



OLS vs Hybrid-GAM: how quickly would each have flagged the COVID break?


Check out the full notebook on GitHub.

title: Reacting to an Exogenous Shock Mid-Forecast: A Demo Model with AEMO Electricity Demand Data
text:
Method
The setup is small enough to read end to end, but it mirrors how AEMO predicts and stress-tests medium-to-long term operational demand.

Data. AEMO operational demand for Victoria, paired with observed weather over the same period, from the beginning of 2017 to the end of 2022. The train set is 2017-2019; the test set is 2020-2022. A true energy demand forecast would draw on more granular time and weather data, plus a bevy of techno-economic variables — this is a bite-sized example dataset.
Two modelling approaches, one question. An Ordinary Least Squares model with a linear temperature response serves as the baseline. OLS measures how an outcome — here, energy demand — reacts linearly to a set of parameters — time and weather. The alternative is a hybrid OLS-GAM: the OLS component still explains time effects linearly, while the GAM (Generalized Additive Model) lets temperature have a nonlinear relationship with demand, so long as that relationship follows a smooth curve. The aim was to separate two things that are easy to conflate — a misspecified weather response and a genuine structural shock.
Uncertainty, the way operators use it. Each model produces POE10 / POE50 / POE90 bands — the demand levels exceeded 10%, 50%, and 90% of the time — built by resampling historical weather years through the fitted model. With a larger training set, this would let you pinpoint the probability of exceeding a given demand level, the same way AEMO frames its own forecasts.
Detecting the break. A structural-break test over the demand series handles the detection half of the question: if you were watching this unfold in real time, when would the data first tell you something had changed — and would it tell you what?
Accounting for that break. How do you tell your model that something unusual is occurring? The most direct way is to give it that information explicitly, as a column that says “Yes, COVID lockdowns are happening” or “No, COVID lockdowns are not happening.” This Yes/No data is integrated into the models as a fixed effect.

title: Reacting to an Exogenous Shock Mid-Forecast: A Demo Model with AEMO Electricity Demand Data
text:
Results
The first break wasn’t COVID. Run break detection over the series and the earliest structural break doesn’t land in March 2020 — it lands months earlier, in the Black Summer bushfire window over the 2019-20 summer. The obvious shock wasn’t the first one. It’s a useful reminder that “the data changed” and “the thing I was worried about happened” are different statements: detection tells you that something shifted, not what.
The nonlinear fix beat the COVID fix. Across the three models, adding an explicit COVID intervention term helped less than simply switching to a nonlinear temperature response. Much of what looked like a COVID anomaly under OLS was really the linear model failing to capture the curve of the temperature-demand relationship at the extremes. Once the GAM handled that, the leftover “COVID effect” shrank substantially.

Check how much the error bars decrease — the difference between the red bar and the blue bar is the degree to which accounting for COVID makes the models better.
The two findings make one practical point: when a shock looks like it’s breaking your forecast, check your functional form before you reach for a special-case term. The dummy you add to “fix COVID” may be quietly compensating for a model that was misspecified the whole time.

title: Reflections on Vibe Coding: Get the Craftsman a Duck that Talks Back
text:
Grace Gordon

    Data Scientist & Data Engineer

    Forecasting outcomes. Modelling risk. Shipping data products that hold up in production.

    

      

      

    

  





  Projects

  Articles

  About












No matter what I did, I couldn’t find an image of a Claude-branded rubber duck. Come on, Anthropic, this is an obvious one.



Image courtesy of rawpixel (CC0).

I have been experimenting with vibe coding recently. I’d be a fool not to. My take? AI’s value depends entirely on what kind of work you’re pointing it at — net-new code vs. legacy/migration.
An article by Business Insider titled “Software engineers are facing an ‘identity crisis bordering on depression,’ Menlo Ventures partner says” states that software engineering teams are splitting into two camps — the camp of “Lazy Engineers,” who produce the code, and the “Craftsmen,” who trail after them, mopping it up. I can sympathize with the Craftsmen here, since reviewing written code is one of the most hated tasks in software engineering. I’m one of the few odd ducks that actually enjoys it.
I recently tried using AI on two projects. First, this website, which was primarily made with the publishing system Quarto. The second was my migration of newsagger, a Python module for querying American newspapers from the Chronicling America collection, where I migrated the existing bulk downloader from the pre-August 2025 Library of Congress API rules to the current standard.
My takeaways from the whole experience:

For the website — loved it. Yes, it was a lot of back and forth of “yes, that looks nice,” and “no, not that.” But to be honest, unless it’s a dashboard I don’t particularly enjoy designing frontends. AI allowed me to quickly finish a project without sinking time into learning a once-off technology (Quarto).
But out-of-the-box AI doesn’t have the discipline — or research skills — to handle a major migration. Migrations of code bases from one standard require a light touch. You need to stick to limited changes. Your superior method of calculating percentage loss, or translating the user’s text query? If even one other function would need updating to accept your change, you’re wasting time. Not to mention you run the risk of confusing your performance changes for the changes required by migration, resulting in one giant confusing mess of spaghetti.
Another little-talked-about task involved in migrating a legacy codebase to a new standard is the research involved. For the migration project, I had to determine how the API query logic for Chronicling America had changed. That meant not only going to Chronicling America’s current website, but spinning up my own Jupyter notebook to test my queries (yes, Postman would have been an option in hindsight — we all have our first instincts), checking the deprecated Chronicling America web portal Chronam, and using the Wayback Machine to access historical query instructions. There are ways of understanding sequences of publishing, and how to look back at old materials. But as of today, your Claude and your Copilot don’t do that.
But it’s better than a rubber ducky. In order to properly migrate the module newsagger, I had to refactor how the module handled API query creation and response interpretation. Before, it was spread throughout the module. I needed to bring it all together so that changes to interpretation could be tracked and better implemented. I liked bouncing ideas off Claude for how to best architect the code. Claude might not have been directly coding in these chats, but it was helpful.

After my experiences, I went and checked the literature on AI’s efficiency contribution to coding. One result straight from the research: in Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity (METR), the productivity gain from AI was tested with 16 developers. Screen recordings found the developers were 19% slower with AI tools — despite predicting a 24% speedup beforehand and still believing afterward they’d been 20% faster. These were developers working on their pre-existing codebases, legacy code and all. In contrast, a separate Copilot RCT — The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot — of developers recruited via Upwork found 55% faster completion on a self-contained, well-defined task — building a JavaScript HTTP server from scratch. My experience may not be a fluke after all.
I’ll continue doing some vibe coding for now. It’s the best way to stay up to date as AI coding changes, after all. But I think for organizations to make the best use of coding assistants, they need to sit down and think about when they add value. Not all projects need AI to directly touch the code. For well-scoped new builds, it’s nice to be lazy. Even isolated code bases with excellent documentation and well-scoped sprints could benefit (let me know if you ever find one of those organizations — I’ve always wanted to go to Heaven). But when we’re touching a mass of legacy code, the complex systems the business has in production? It may be that the AI does not need to directly touch the code. After all, Craftsmen don’t need a ghostwriter for what they’re doing. They need a duck that talks back.

Related project: Chronicling America API Migration

title: About

Data Scientist & Data Engineer

title: About
text:
Grace Gordon




I like building fun things with real data. My whole career has been about working with people to make that happen.
I’m a Principal Data Scientist & Data Engineer with 10+ years leading data, analytics, and AI delivery across banking, infrastructure, defence, and government. I build the pipelines, manage the platforms, and build and deploy the models — and I also align stakeholders, design solutions, and lead delivery. I like being the person in the middle: moving between strategy discussions, architecture diagrams, and production code.
Most of my work has been in high-risk, highly regulated environments. My approach is collaborative and practical, built around a few simple principles: clean code, good data hygiene, clear communication, and understanding the consequences of the decisions we’re making.
I also have a not-so-secret fascination with graph databases and graph analytics. Lately, I’ve been exploring how knowledge graphs and generative AI can work together to improve both governance and outcomes. Good AI starts with good information architecture.
Welcome to my portfolio, where the strategic, the serious, and the occasionally-just-for-fun all live side by side.


How I Work

I prefer an email over a meeting.
I prefer a short word over a long one.
I’d rather be in the room with the people who know the domain than off building in a silo — their context makes my work better.
I enjoy mentoring and will almost always make time to talk data.
I like solving problems that actually matter and delivering outcomes people can see.



Skills

Data Engineering & Analytics: Python, SQL, PySpark, Databricks, Microsoft Fabric, ETL, Data Pipelines, Data Architecture
Machine Learning, Statistics & AI: Forecasting, Statistical Modeling, Predictive Modeling, Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence (AI), Generative AI, Large Language Models (LLM), Natural Language Processing (NLP), Simio
Data Systems & Graphs: Neo4j, Cypher, Graph Databases, Knowledge Graphs, Cassandra
Business Intelligence: Microsoft Power BI, Qlik
Cloud Platforms: Microsoft Azure
Leadership & Delivery: Technical Leadership, Stakeholder Management, Vendor Management, Budget Management, Mentoring, Project Delivery, Data Governance, Regulatory Compliance
Tools & Collaboration: Git, JIRA, Confluence



Contact

Email
GitHub
LinkedIn
